It introduces more programming effort than necessary, violating the requirement for minimal programming.

questions.question: Scenario: You are tasked with designing a data warehouse in BigQuery to analyze sales data for a telecommunication service provider. The data model needs to include customers, products, and subscriptions, with the requirement to maintain a historical record of all data that can be updated monthly. The visualization layer will be used for both current and historical reporting. What steps should you take to ensure that the data model is simple, user-friendly, and cost-effective for analyzing sales data in BigQuery?
options: Create a normalized model with tables for each entity. Use snapshots before updates to track historical data.
Create a normalized model with tables for each entity. Keep all input files in a Cloud Storage bucket to track historical data.
Create a denormalized model with nested and repeated fields. Update the table and use snapshots to track historical data.
Create a denormalized, append-only model with nested and repeated fields. Use the ingestion timestamp to track historical data.
answer: Create a denormalized, append-only model with nested and repeated fields. Use the ingestion timestamp to track historical data.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D: Create a denormalized, append-only model with nested and repeated fields. Use the ingestion timestamp to track historical data.– In this scenario, it is important to maintain a historical record of all data while keeping the data model simple, easy-to-use, and cost-effective.– Creating a denormalized, append-only model with nested and repeated fields allows for a simpler data structure that is easier to query and analyze.– Using the ingestion timestamp to track historical data enables you to easily identify the point in time when each record was added to the data warehouse, thus maintaining a historical record of data changes.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Create a normalized model with tables for each entity. Use snapshots before updates to track historical data.– Normalized models with separate tables for each entity can be complex and may require joining tables for analysis, which can be less efficient.– Using snapshots before updates to track historical data can be cumbersome and may not be as straightforward as using an append-only model.B. Create a normalized model with tables for each entity. Keep all input files in a Cloud Storage bucket to track historical data.– Storing input files in a Cloud Storage bucket may not be an efficient way to track historical data within a data warehouse.– Normalized models may not be the best choice for maintaining historical records while ensuring simplicity and cost-effectiveness.C. Create a denormalized model with nested and repeated fields. Update the table and use snapshots to track historical data.– While using a denormalized model with nested and repeated fields is a step in the right direction for simplicity, using snapshots to track historical data may not be as efficient as using an append-only model with ingestion timestamps.– Updating the table directly may overwrite previous data, making it challenging to maintain an accurate historical record.

questions.question: Scenario: Your business users, who are not very tech-savvy, require a user-friendly tool to clean and prepare data for analysis using graphical interfaces. They also prefer to conduct analysis directly in a spreadsheet after data transformation. What solution would you recommend for your less technically savvy business users to clean, prepare data, and perform analysis directly in a spreadsheet?
options: Use Dataprep to clean the data, and write the results to BigQuery. Analyze the data by using Connected Sheets.
Use Dataprep to clean the data, and write the results to BigQuery. Analyze the data by using Looker Studio.
Use Dataflow to clean the data, and write the results to BigQuery. Analyze the data by using Connected Sheets.
Use Dataflow to clean the data, and write the results to BigQuery. Analyze the data by using Looker Studio.
answer: Use Dataprep to clean the data, and write the results to BigQuery. Analyze the data by using Connected Sheets.
questions.explanation: A. Use Dataprep to clean the data, and write the results to BigQuery. Analyze the data by using Connected Sheets.– Dataprep is a data preparation tool that allows users to visually explore, clean, and prepare data without needing to write code. This is suitable for business users who are less technically savvy and prefer working with graphical user interfaces.– BigQuery is a serverless, highly scalable, and cost-effective data warehouse for storing and analyzing large datasets. Writing the cleaned data to BigQuery ensures that the transformed data is stored securely and can be easily accessed for analysis.– Connected Sheets is a feature in Google Sheets that allows users to analyze BigQuery data directly within a spreadsheet environment. This is ideal for business users who want to perform their analysis directly in a spreadsheet without needing to switch between different tools.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Using Looker Studio for analysis is not ideal for less technically savvy business users who prefer graphical user interfaces. Looker Studio is a business intelligence platform that may require more technical knowledge to use effectively.C. Dataflow is a tool for processing and transferring data in real-time, but it may be too technical for business users who prefer graphical user interfaces. Connecting Dataflow to Connected Sheets directly is not a straightforward process for less technically savvy users.D. Similar to option C, using Looker Studio for analysis may be too complex for business users who are less technically savvy and prefer graphical user interfaces. Dataflow for data cleaning may also be overly technical for their needs.

questions.question: Scenario: You use a SQL-based tool to visualize data stored in BigQuery, requiring outer joins and analytic functions. The visualizations need to be based on data at least 4 hours old. Business users are unhappy with the slow generation of visualizations. How can you enhance the performance of visualization queries while reducing the maintenance burden of the data preparation pipeline?
options: Create materialized views with the allow_non_incremental_definition option set to true for the visualization queries. Specify the max_staleness parameter to 4 hours and the enable_refresh parameter to true. Reference the materialized views in the data visualization tool.
Create views for the visualization queries. Reference the views in the data visualization tool.

Create a Cloud Function instance to export the visualization query results as parquet files to a Cloud Storage bucket. Use Cloud Scheduler to trigger the Cloud Function every 4 hours. Reference the parquet files in the data visualization tool.

Create materialized views for the visualization queries. Use the incremental updates capability of BigQuery materialized views to handle changed data automatically. Reference the materialized views in the data visualization tool.
answer: Create materialized views with the allow_non_incremental_definition option set to true for the visualization queries. Specify the max_staleness parameter to 4 hours and the enable_refresh parameter to true. Reference the materialized views in the data visualization tool.
questions.explanation: A. Create materialized views with the allow_non_incremental_definition option set to true for the visualization queries. Specify the max_staleness parameter to 4 hours and the enable_refresh parameter to true. Reference the materialized views in the data visualization tool.Creating materialized views with the allow_non_incremental_definition option set to true allows you to refresh the data in the materialized views without having to rely on incremental updates. By setting the max_staleness parameter to 4 hours, you ensure that the data in the materialized views is no less than 4 hours old, as required. Enabling the refresh parameter ensures that the materialized views are automatically refreshed at the specified interval. This approach improves query performance by precomputing and storing the results of the visualization queries, thereby reducing the query processing time when generating the visualizations.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Creating views for the visualization queries does not address the performance issue caused by the slow generation of visualizations. Views do not store precomputed results and will still require processing the underlying data each time the visualization is generated, leading to slow performance.C. Exporting query results as parquet files using Cloud Functions and Cloud Scheduler introduces unnecessary complexity to the data visualization process. This approach requires additional steps to export, store, and manage the data files, which could increase maintenance overhead and may not necessarily improve query performance.D. While creating materialized views for visualization queries is a step in the right direction, relying on the incremental updates capability of BigQuery materialized views may not be the most efficient solution in this case. Incremental updates require tracking changes in the underlying data, which could introduce complexity and potentially impact performance. Using the approach in option A provides a simpler and more straightforward solution for improving query performance while meeting the data freshness requirement.

questions.question: Scenario: Your company is transitioning from on-premises data warehousing solutions to BigQuery. The current data warehouse utilizes trigger-based change data capture (CDC) to incorporate updates from various transactional databases daily. The goal now is to enhance CDC processes in BigQuery, allowing real-time access to changes from source systems through log-based CDC streams. The objective is to optimize performance in applying these changes to the data warehouse. To achieve minimal latency in making changes available in the BigQuery reporting table and reduce compute overhead, what are the two recommended steps that should be implemented? (Choose two.)
options: Perform a DML INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE to replicate each individual CDC record in real time directly on the reporting table.
Insert each new CDC record and corresponding operation type to a staging table in real time.
Periodically DELETE outdated records from the reporting table.
Periodically use a DML MERGE to perform several DML INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations at the same time on the reporting table.
Insert each new CDC record and corresponding operation type in real time to the reporting table, and use a materialized view to expose only the newest version of each unique record.
answer: Insert each new CDC record and corresponding operation type to a staging table in real time.
Periodically use a DML MERGE to perform several DML INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations at the same time on the reporting table.
questions.explanation: BD is the correct answer for the question.– Option B: Inserting each new CDC record and corresponding operation type to a staging table in real time allows for minimal latency as changes are captured immediately. This approach helps reduce compute overhead by processing changes in batches rather than individually, optimizing performance.– Option D: Periodically using a DML MERGE to perform several DML INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations at the same time on the reporting table helps manage changes efficiently and reduce compute overhead by consolidating multiple operations into a single transaction.Explanation of why the other options are incorrect:– Option A: Performing a DML INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE to replicate each individual CDC record in real time directly on the reporting table can lead to high compute overhead and increased latency, as each record is processed individually.– Option C: Periodically deleting outdated records from the reporting table can introduce latency and additional processing overhead, especially if the deletion process is not optimized for performance.– Option E: Inserting each new CDC record and corresponding operation type in real time to the reporting table and using a materialized view to expose only the newest version of each unique record may not be the most efficient approach for minimizing latency and reducing compute overhead, as it involves additional steps and complexity in managing the data.

questions.question: Scenario: You are tasked with setting access to BigQuery for various departments in your company. The solution should adhere to specific criteria: – Each department should access only their data. – Each department will have one or more leads who can create and update tables for their team. – Each department includes data analysts who can query data but not modify it. How would you configure access to the data in BigQuery to meet the specified requirements?
options: Create a dataset for each department. Assign the department leads the role of OWNER, and assign the data analysts the role of WRITER on their dataset.
Create a dataset for each department. Assign the department leads the role of WRITER, and assign the data analysts the role of READER on their dataset.
Create a table for each department. Assign the department leads the role of Owner, and assign the data analysts the role of Editor on the project the table is in.
Create a table for each department. Assign the department leads the role of Editor, and assign the data analysts the role of Viewer on the project the table is in.
answer: Create a dataset for each department. Assign the department leads the role of WRITER, and assign the data analysts the role of READER on their dataset.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option B: Create a dataset for each department. Assign the department leads the role of WRITER, and assign the data analysts the role of READER on their dataset.1. Creating a dataset for each department allows you to segregate the data and provide access control at a more granular level.2. Assigning the department leads the role of WRITER allows them to create and update tables within their dataset, which aligns with the requirement that they should be able to provide data to their team.3. Assigning the data analysts the role of READER ensures that they can query the data but not modify it, meeting the requirement that they should be able to query but not modify data.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Create a dataset for each department. Assign the department leads the role of OWNER, and assign the data analysts the role of WRITER on their dataset.– Assigning the department leads the role of OWNER would give them full control over the dataset, including the ability to modify access control settings, which is not necessary based on the requirements.– Assigning the data analysts the role of WRITER would allow them to modify the data, which is not in line with the requirement that they should only be able to query the data.C. Create a table for each department. Assign the department leads the role of Owner, and assign the data analysts the role of Editor on the project the table is in.– Creating a table for each department does not provide the necessary level of segregation required by the departments.– Assigning the department leads the role of OWNER would give them unnecessary permissions, as they only need to be able to create and update tables.– Assigning the data analysts the role of EDITOR on the project would allow them to modify the data, which is not in line with the requirement that they should only be able to query the data.D. Create a table for each department. Assign the department leads the role of Editor, and assign the data analysts the role of Viewer on the project the table is in.– Similar to option C, creating tables for each department does not provide the necessary level of segregation required by the departments.– Assigning the department leads the role of EDITOR would give them unnecessary permissions, as they only need to be able to create and update tables.– Assigning the data analysts the role of VIEWER would restrict them from querying the data, which is not in line with the requirement that they should be able to query the data.

questions.question: Scenario: You have opted to utilize Cloud Datastore for processing real-time vehicle telemetry data. You aim to establish a storage solution that can handle the continuous data expansion efficiently without incurring high expenses. Additionally, you intend to generate periodic snapshots of the data to facilitate point-in-time recovery and data cloning for Cloud Datastore across diverse environments. Your objective is to archive these snapshots for an extended duration. Which two approaches can fulfill the requirements mentioned above in managing the data snapshots effectively for Cloud Datastore? (Choose Two)
options: Use managed export, and store the data in a Cloud Storage bucket using Nearline or Coldline class.
Use managed export, and then import to Cloud Datastore in a separate project under a unique namespace reserved for that export.
Use managed export, and then import the data into a BigQuery table created just for that export, and delete temporary export files.

Write an application that uses Cloud Datastore client libraries to read all the entities. Treat each entity as a BigQuery table row via BigQuery streaming insert. Assign an export timestamp for each export, and attach it as an extra column for each row. Make sure that the BigQuery table is partitioned using the export timestamp column.

Write an application that uses Cloud Datastore client libraries to read all the entities. Format the exported data into a JSON file. Apply compression before storing the data in Cloud Source Repositories.
answer: Use managed export, and store the data in a Cloud Storage bucket using Nearline or Coldline class.
Use managed export, and then import to Cloud Datastore in a separate project under a unique namespace reserved for that export.
questions.explanation: Option A (Use managed export, and store the data in a Cloud Storage bucket using Nearline or Coldline class) is the correct answer for the question. This method allows you to export snapshots of the data from Cloud Datastore and store them in a Cloud Storage bucket using Nearline or Coldline storage classes. Nearline and Coldline are cost-effective storage classes designed for data that is accessed less frequently but needs to be retained for long-term archival purposes. By using managed export and storing the data in a Cloud Storage bucket, you can achieve both long-term data retention and cost-effectiveness.Option B (Use managed export, and then import to Cloud Datastore in a separate project under a unique namespace reserved for that export) is correct because importing the data back into Cloud Datastore in a separate project with a unique namespace is a suitable method for creating snapshots for long-term archival purposes.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:Option C (Use managed export, and then import the data into a BigQuery table created just for that export, and delete temporary export files) is incorrect because importing the data into a BigQuery table is not an appropriate method for creating snapshots of the data for long-term archival purposes. BigQuery is a data warehouse solution, not a storage solution for long-term data retention.Option D (Write an application that uses Cloud Datastore client libraries to read all the entities and treat each entity as a BigQuery table row) is incorrect because this option involves converting the data into BigQuery tables and partitioning them, which is not necessary for creating snapshots for long-term data archival. This method introduces unnecessary complexity and does not align with the requirements.Option E (Write an application that uses Cloud Datastore client libraries to read all the entities, format the exported data into a JSON file, and store the data in Cloud Source Repositories) is incorrect because storing the data in Cloud Source Repositories is not intended for long-term data archival. Cloud Source Repositories is primarily used for source code version control, not for storing large amounts of data for archival purposes.

questions.question: Scenario: You are migrating a table to BigQuery and considering the data model. The table contains data on purchases made at various store locations, including transaction time, items purchased, store ID, and store location details like city and state. Queries are often run to analyze item sales over the last 30 days and to examine purchasing trends by state, city, and store. How should you structure the table to optimize query performance for analyzing item sales and purchasing trends by state, city, and individual store in BigQuery?
options: Partition by transaction time; cluster by state first, then city, then store ID.
Partition by transaction time; cluster by store ID first, then city, then state.
Top-level cluster by state first, then city, then store ID.
Top-level cluster by store ID first, then city, then state.
answer: Partition by transaction time; cluster by state first, then city, then store ID.
questions.explanation: A. Partition by transaction time; cluster by state first, then city, then store ID is the correct answer for the best query performance in this scenario.– Partitioning by transaction time will help in organizing the data based on time intervals, making it easier to query and analyze data for specific time periods.– Clustering by state first, then city, then store ID will group similar data together physically on disk, which can significantly improve query performance for common queries that involve filtering by state, city, and store ID.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Partition by transaction time; cluster by store ID first, then city, then state:– Clustering by store ID first may not be as effective for the given query patterns because most queries are based on analyzing trends by state, city, and individual store. This clustering order may not optimize the performance for such queries.C. Top-level cluster by state first, then city, then store ID:– Clustering at the top-level by state, city, and store ID without partitioning by transaction time may not be efficient for time-based queries. It is important to partition the data by transaction time to optimize query performance for time-based analysis.D. Top-level cluster by store ID first, then city, then state:– Similar to option B, clustering by store ID first may not be the best approach for queries that involve analyzing trends by state, city, and individual store. This clustering order may not provide optimal performance for the given query patterns.

questions.question: Scenario: Your company is transitioning to BigQuery for a data warehouse project and you are in charge of designing the data model. After migrating your on-premises sales data warehouse with a star data schema to BigQuery, you encounter performance challenges when querying data from the last 30 days. Following Google‘s best practices, how can you enhance query performance without raising storage expenses?
options: Denormalize the data.
Shard the data by customer ID.
Materialize the dimensional data in views.
Partition the data by transaction date.
answer: Partition the data by transaction date.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Partition the data by transaction date.Partitioning the data by transaction date in BigQuery allows you to divide the data into smaller, manageable parts based on the transaction date. This helps in optimizing query performance as BigQuery can scan only the relevant partitions when querying data for a specific date range. By partitioning the data, you can speed up the query without increasing storage costs.Now let‘s discuss why the other options are incorrect:A. Denormalize the data:Denormalizing the data involves combining tables and duplicating data to reduce the number of joins required in queries. While denormalization can improve query performance in some cases, it may not be the most efficient solution in this scenario. Denormalization can lead to increased storage costs and complexity in maintaining the data model.B. Shard the data by customer ID:Sharding the data by customer ID involves splitting the data based on customer IDs to distribute the data across multiple nodes. While sharding can help in scaling out data storage and processing, it may not directly address the performance issues related to querying data for the past 30 days. Sharding by customer ID is more suitable for scenarios where you need to distribute data across multiple servers for scalability.C. Materialize the dimensional data in views:Materializing the dimensional data in views involves pre-computing and storing the results of complex queries in views. While materialized views can improve query performance by reducing the need to recompute results each time a query is run, it may not be the most effective solution for speeding up queries related to the past 30 days of data. Materialized views can also increase storage costs and maintenance overhead.In conclusion, partitioning the data by transaction date is the recommended approach in this scenario to speed up queries without increasing storage costs in BigQuery.

questions.question: Scenario: You are transferring an application that manages library books and their details like author and year of publication from an on-premises data warehouse to BigQuery. In the existing relational database, author information is stored in a separate table and linked to book information through a common key. According to Google‘s schema design best practices, how should you organize the data to guarantee fast query performance for retrieving information about the authors of borrowed books?
options: Keep the schema the same, maintain the different tables for the book and each of the attributes, and query as you are doing today.
Create a table that is wide and includes a column for each attribute, including the author‘s first name, last name, date of birth, etc.
Create a table that includes information about the books and authors, but nest the author fields inside the author column.
Keep the schema the same, create a view that joins all of the tables, and always query the view.
answer: Create a table that includes information about the books and authors, but nest the author fields inside the author column.
questions.explanation: C is the correct answer for the question. By creating a table that includes information about both the books and authors and nesting the author fields inside the author column, you can ensure optimal speed of queries about the author of each book that has been borrowed in BigQuery. This denormalized approach is recommended by Google for BigQuery schema design as it reduces the need for joins, which can slow down query performance in a distributed system like BigQuery.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Keeping the schema the same and maintaining separate tables for books and authors would require performing joins in queries, which can be less efficient in a distributed system like BigQuery. This approach would not ensure optimal speed of queries about the author of each book.B. Creating a wide table with a column for each attribute, including author details, would result in redundant data storage and could lead to data inconsistency if author details change. This approach is not recommended as it does not follow best practices for schema design in BigQuery.D. Keeping the schema the same and creating a view that joins all the tables would still require performing joins in queries, which can impact query performance in BigQuery. Views can also introduce additional complexity and overhead to query processing. This approach would not ensure optimal speed of queries about the author of each book.

questions.question: Scenario: In a report-only data warehouse, data is streamed into BigQuery through the streaming API. To adhere to Google‘s best practices, there are separate staging and production tables for the data. How should the data loading be designed to maintain a single master dataset without impacting performance in either the ingestion or reporting aspects?
options: Have a staging table that is an append-only model, and then update the production table every three hours with the changes written to staging.
Have a staging table that is an append-only model, and then update the production table every ninety minutes with the changes written to staging.
Have a staging table that moves the staged data over to the production table and deletes the contents of the staging table every three hours.
Have a staging table that moves the staged data over to the production table and deletes the contents of the staging table every thirty minutes.
answer: Have a staging table that moves the staged data over to the production table and deletes the contents of the staging table every three hours.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C: Have a staging table that moves the staged data over to the production table and deletes the contents of the staging table every three hours.– By moving the staged data over to the production table and deleting the contents of the staging table every three hours, you ensure that the production table always contains the most up-to-date data without affecting performance. This approach allows for a consistent and efficient data loading process.Why the other options are incorrect:A. Having a staging table that is an append-only model and updating the production table every three hours with the changes would result in potential performance issues, as updating the production table frequently can impact query performance.B. Updating the production table every ninety minutes with the changes written to staging may be too frequent and could lead to performance degradation on both the ingestion and reporting pieces.D. Deleting the contents of the staging table every thirty minutes is too frequent and may result in data loss or inconsistency if there are any issues during the data loading process. This approach could also impact performance due to the high frequency of data movements.

questions.question: Scenario: Your new customer needs daily reports to track their net consumption of Google Cloud compute resources and identify the users utilizing the resources. How can you efficiently and promptly create the required daily reports for your customer?
options: Do daily exports of Cloud Logging data to BigQuery. Create views filtering by project, log type, resource, and user.
Filter data in Cloud Logging by project, resource, and user; then export the data in CSV format.
Filter data in Cloud Logging by project, log type, resource, and user, then import the data into BigQuery.
Export Cloud Logging data to Cloud Storage in CSV format. Cleanse the data using Dataprep, filtering by project, resource, and user.
answer: Do daily exports of Cloud Logging data to BigQuery. Create views filtering by project, log type, resource, and user.
questions.explanation: A. Do daily exports of Cloud Logging data to BigQuery. Create views filtering by project, log type, resource, and user.– By exporting Cloud Logging data to BigQuery, you can efficiently store and analyze the data in a scalable and cost-effective manner.– Creating views in BigQuery allows you to filter the data based on specific criteria such as project, log type, resource, and user, which is essential for generating the daily reports requested by the customer.– BigQuery‘s querying capabilities enable you to process large datasets quickly and provide insights into resource consumption and usage.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Filter data in Cloud Logging by project, resource, and user; then export the data in CSV format.– This approach may not be efficient for generating daily reports as it involves manual filtering and exporting data in CSV format, which may not scale well for large datasets.– Exporting data in CSV format may limit your ability to perform advanced analytics and filtering compared to using a data warehouse like BigQuery.C. Filter data in Cloud Logging by project, log type, resource, and user, then import the data into BigQuery.– This option involves unnecessary steps of filtering and importing data into BigQuery manually, which can be time-consuming and less efficient compared to directly exporting data to BigQuery.D. Export Cloud Logging data to Cloud Storage in CSV format. Cleanse the data using Dataprep, filtering by project, resource, and user.– This option introduces additional complexity by exporting data to Cloud Storage, then cleansing and filtering the data using Dataprep before generating reports.– Using BigQuery directly for filtering and analysis would be more straightforward and efficient for the task at hand.

questions.question: Scenario: You have migrated all your data into tables in a dataset in BigQuery for the data warehouse migration. Multiple users from your organization will access the data and should only see specific tables based on their team membership. How should you configure user permissions to achieve this in BigQuery?
options: Assign the users/groups data viewer access at the table level for each table
Create SQL views for each team in the same dataset in which the data resides, and assign the users/groups data viewer access to the SQL views
Create authorized views for each team in the same dataset in which the data resides, and assign the users/groups data viewer access to the authorized views

Create authorized views for each team in datasets created for each team. Assign the authorized views data viewer access to the dataset in which the data resides. Assign the users/groups data viewer access to the datasets in which the authorized views reside
answer: Create authorized views for each team in the same dataset in which the data resides, and assign the users/groups data viewer access to the authorized views
questions.explanation: Correct Option C. Create authorized views for each team in the same dataset in which the data resides, and assign the users/groups data viewer access to the authorized views Correct. In BigQuery, authorized views allow you to restrict access to specific subsets of data. You grant the view access to the underlying dataset, and then assign users/groups access to the view. This ensures that users only see the data exposed through the authorized view, not the entire dataset. Incorrect Option A. Assign data viewer access at the table level Incorrect. While you can grant table‑level permissions, this approach is not scalable for multiple teams and does not provide fine‑grained control over subsets of data. Authorized views are the recommended method. Option B. Create SQL views and assign access Incorrect. Standard SQL views alone do not enforce security. Users with access to the view must also have access to the underlying tables. Without authorized views, this does not restrict data properly. Option D. Create authorized views in separate datasets and assign access Incorrect. While technically possible, this adds unnecessary complexity. You don’t need separate datasets per team; authorized views within the same dataset are sufficient and best practice.
